patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 52-year-old male regional sales manager who travels a lot. You are vague and evasive. Start with: “I just can’t sleep, and it’s starting to mess up my life.” Volunteer only: takes hours to fall asleep, wakes many times and can’t get back to sleep, feels wiped out/foggy next day, worsening for a couple months. If asked, add: racing work thoughts, early awakening, irritability, more coffee, occasional OTC sleep aids (groggy), sometimes heart pounding, use phone at night.
Only reveal hidden items if specifically prompted: occasional snoring (worse on back), dry mouth, dull morning headache, nocturia 1–2x, alcohol worsens sleep, higher sugars/thirst, little daylight/exercise.
Resist disclosing unless directly asked about substances/mental health: 2 drinks nightly to “knock out,” sometimes a friend’s anti-anxiety pill, feeling down/hopeless, occasional panicky awakening from bad dreams.
Express misconceptions: worry diabetes nerve damage (tingling), thyroid/adrenal fatigue, “low sugar” at night.
Push for strong/brand-name meds and quick fixes; dismiss CBT-I/behavior changes as a waste. If clinician shows empathy, briefly soften but still ask for a prescription.

medications: Secobarbital 100 mg PO at bedtime as needed for sleep
Glipizide ER 5 mg PO once daily with breakfast
Lisinopril 10 mg PO once daily
confounders: Obstructive sleep apnea risk (male, BMI 29.4, reports loud snoring and nonrestorative sleep)
High caffeine intake (3-5 cups coffee daily, last intake mid-afternoon)
Evening alcohol use (2-3 drinks most nights, worsens sleep maintenance)
Shift-work/irregular sleep schedule (rotating on-call nights)
Anxiety symptoms related to work stress (rumination at bedtime)

acceptable_alternatives: Insomnia disorder due to medication/substance (sedative-hypnotic related)
Circadian rhythm sleep-wake disorder (if history supports)
required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep, maintaining sleep, and/or early morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Occurs at least 3 nights per week (or clinically significant recurrent pattern) and causes daytime impairment (fatigue, impaired concentration, mood disturbance)
Adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep
Not better explained by another sleep disorder (e.g., OSA, RLS), medical/psychiatric condition, or substance use
Medication history including current/recent use of secobarbital and assessment for tolerance/dependence/withdrawal contributing to sleep complaint

expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: Typically normal in primary insomnia; abnormal values would prompt evaluation for metabolic contributors (e.g., diabetes) rather than confirm insomnia
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Typically normal in primary insomnia; elevated suggests comorbid diabetes that may worsen sleep
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - Sodium: Typically normal; hyponatremia/hypernatremia would suggest alternative contributors to symptoms
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - BUN: Typically normal; elevation may indicate renal disease contributing to sleep disturbance
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - Creatinine: Typically normal; elevation may indicate renal disease contributing to sleep disturbance

medications: Melatonin 1-3 mg PO nightly 1-2 hours before bedtime (if sleep-onset insomnia or circadian component suspected)
Doxepin 3 mg PO nightly (for sleep-maintenance insomnia; avoid if significant anticholinergic sensitivity)
non_pharmacological: First-line: Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) with stimulus control, sleep restriction, and cognitive restructuring
Sleep hygiene: consistent wake time, limit naps, avoid caffeine after noon, avoid alcohol near bedtime, wind-down routine, reduce evening screen exposure
Assess and treat contributing factors: pain, nocturia, anxiety/depression, thyroid symptoms, GERD, and screen for OSA/RLS based on history
If currently taking secobarbital: assess for dependence; avoid abrupt discontinuation—plan supervised taper and/or addiction medicine consultation as indicated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 2-4 weeks to assess response to CBT-I and/or medication, daytime functioning, adverse effects, and need for tapering sedative-hypnotics

must_include: Define insomnia and confirm it is based on symptoms plus daytime impairment
Explain common contributors and that labs are mainly to rule out other causes, not to diagnose insomnia
Discuss risks of barbiturates (secobarbital): tolerance, dependence, rebound insomnia, overdose risk
CBT-I is first-line and often more effective long-term than sedatives
If medication is used, it should be lowest effective dose and short-term with monitoring
Avoid combining secobarbital with alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, or other sedating medications due to respiratory depression/overdose risk
If on secobarbital, do not stop suddenly; outline a taper/monitoring plan
Elicit patient goals and concerns (e.g., fear of not sleeping, reliance on secobarbital)
Review adverse effects to watch for (next-day sedation, falls, confusion, complex sleep behaviors if sedatives used)
Shared decision-making on CBT-I referral and medication options
Consistent sleep-wake schedule and stimulus control (bed only for sleep/sex; leave bed if awake >20 minutes)
Limit caffeine/nicotine and avoid alcohol near bedtime
Regular daytime exercise (not close to bedtime) and light exposure in the morning

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit insomnia complaint details, establish chronicity, daytime impairment, and screen for red flags/other sleep disorders and contributing factors.
Order targeted tests only if indicated to rule out contributory medical conditions (e.g., TSH for thyroid disease, ferritin if RLS suspected), not to diagnose insomnia.
Review results to refine differential and ensure no secondary cause requires treatment first.
Make insomnia diagnosis based on sleep difficulty plus daytime impairment and adequate opportunity for sleep; document subtype (acute vs chronic) and contributors.
Explain diagnosis and contributors; counsel that CBT-I is first-line; provide sleep hygiene/stimulus control recommendations; discuss risks of barbiturates (secobarbital).
Before any prescribing, confirm allergies/intolerances to proposed medications and relevant classes.
Before prescribing, check for interactions, especially with alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, antihistamines, and other CNS depressants.
Before prescribing, assess contraindications (e.g., respiratory disease/sleep apnea risk, pregnancy, substance use disorder risk, hepatic impairment, fall risk/elderly).

If medication is used, choose lowest effective dose for short term with monitoring; avoid/strongly discourage secobarbital due to dependence/overdose risk; if already on secobarbital, plan taper and do not stop abruptly.
Arrange follow-up to assess response, adverse effects, adherence to CBT-I, and to adjust/taper any sedatives.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent defines insomnia as difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep or early awakening with adequate opportunity, and confirms clinically significant daytime impairment before diagnosing.

Agent screens for common contributors and alternate diagnoses: stress/anxiety/depression, substance use (caffeine/nicotine/alcohol), medications, pain, GERD, circadian rhythm disorder, restless legs symptoms, and obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness).
Agent states that labs are not required to diagnose insomnia and are used selectively to rule out other causes when clinically indicated.
Agent recommends CBT-I as first-line and explains it is often more effective long-term than sedatives.

Agent explicitly discusses barbiturate (secobarbital) risks: tolerance, dependence, rebound insomnia, cognitive impairment, falls, and high overdose/respiratory depression risk.
Agent warns not to combine secobarbital (or other sedatives) with alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, or other sedating medications due to additive respiratory depression/overdose risk.
If patient is currently using secobarbital, agent advises against abrupt discontinuation and outlines a taper/monitoring plan and/or referral for supervised tapering.

Agent reviews adverse effects to watch for: next-day sedation, impaired driving, falls, confusion, and complex sleep behaviors (if sedative-hypnotics are used).

Agent provides lifestyle and behavioral education: consistent sleep-wake schedule, stimulus control (bed only for sleep/sex; leave bed if awake >20 minutes), limit caffeine/nicotine, avoid alcohol near bedtime, regular daytime exercise not near bedtime, and morning light exposure.
Agent completes critical safety checks (allergy, interaction, contraindication) before any prescribing tool call; failure is a critical error.

Agent safety-nets for red flags (suicidality, severe depression/mania, signs of sleep apnea with high risk, substance misuse, severe daytime sleepiness with driving risk) and escalates/refers appropriately.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male construction project supervisor. You speak clearly but act anxious and worried about cost and missing work. Start by saying you keep getting brutal one-sided headaches around your left eye/temple that come in attacks. Volunteer: severe sudden pain in waves, left eye red/watering, same-side runny/stuffy nose, and you can’t sit still (pace/rock). If asked, give details: attacks last 45 min–2 hrs, 1–3/day during bad periods, often wake you 1–2 hrs after sleep, occur in clusters for weeks then months off, little nausea/light/sound sensitivity, occasional eyelid droop, alcohol triggers during clusters, family member similar, and they hit at similar times daily. Only if specifically asked, reveal: keyed-up feeling before attacks, fear of sleep, OTC meds don’t work and you sometimes exceed label, avoiding social plans. Be reluctant but honest if asked: you smoke more during clusters, tried a friend’s strong pain pills, and had panicky ‘can’t live with this pain’ thoughts but deny intent to self-harm. Mention you suspect trigeminal neuralgia/tooth/jaw issue due to electric jabs and chewing discomfort. If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and admit you’re scared and need a plan that’s affordable and won’t require clinic visits.

medications: Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily
Nicotine transdermal patch 21 mg/24 hr apply daily (intermittent use)
confounders: Chronic tobacco use (increases cluster headache risk and can mimic sinus-related complaints)
Seasonal allergic rhinitis with nasal congestion/tearing (can be mistaken for sinusitis during attacks)
Alcohol reliably triggers attacks during active bouts (may be misattributed to hangover or migraine triggers)
Family history of severe migraine in mother (overlapping features and medication history can obscure diagnosis)

required_evidence: Recurrent attacks of severe or very severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes (untreated)
Attack frequency from 1 every other day up to 8 per day, often in bouts with circadian/circannual pattern

Ipsilateral cranial autonomic features during attacks (e.g., lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis) and/or prominent restlessness/agitation
Clinical features favor cluster headache over trigeminal neuralgia: pain not brief electric-shock seconds-long, not triggered by touch/chewing, and presence of autonomic symptoms/restlessness

medications: Oxygen 100% 12-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask for 15-20 minutes at onset of attack PRN
Sumatriptan 6 mg subcutaneous at onset of attack; may repeat once after 1 hour; max 12 mg/day
Verapamil immediate-release 80 mg PO three times daily; titrate every 7-14 days as tolerated/effective (with ECG monitoring)
Prednisone 60 mg PO daily for 5 days then taper by 10 mg every 2 days (bridge while preventive therapy takes effect)
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during cluster period (common trigger)
Sleep regularity and avoidance of known triggers (e.g., strong odors/solvents, nitroglycerin exposure)
Headache diary documenting timing, duration, autonomic symptoms, treatments used and response
Consider neurology/headache specialist referral for preventive titration and alternatives (e.g., galcanezumab, lithium, topiramate) if refractory
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks to assess response, adverse effects, and titrate preventive therapy; sooner if red flags or worsening

must_include: Diagnosis is cluster headache (a trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia), not a dangerous brain condition based on typical pattern and autonomic symptoms
How cluster differs from trigeminal neuralgia (duration minutes-hours vs seconds; autonomic symptoms/restlessness; not primarily touch-triggered)
Expected course: occurs in bouts; familial tendency possible
Acute plan: high-flow oxygen and/or injectable sumatriptan at the start of an attack
Preventive plan: verapamil daily to reduce attack frequency; needs dose titration and ECG monitoring for heart rhythm/PR prolongation
Bridge therapy: short steroid taper to reduce attacks while verapamil starts working
Clear limits and instructions for triptan use (max daily dose; when to seek care)
Screen for cardiovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, stroke history, hemiplegic/basilar migraine features before triptan use
Review side effects: sumatriptan (chest tightness, flushing), verapamil (constipation, dizziness, bradycardia), steroids (insomnia, mood changes, hyperglycemia)
Red flags requiring urgent evaluation: new neurologic deficits, fever/meningismus, sudden thunderclap onset, new headache after age >50, immunosuppression/cancer, persistent atypical symptoms
Avoid alcohol during cluster periods and identify personal triggers
Use headache diary and bring to follow-up
Importance of adherence to preventive therapy and follow-up for ECG/lab monitoring as indicated

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit headache phenotype to confirm cluster pattern and exclude secondary causes: unilateral orbital/temporal severe pain, duration 15–180 min, frequency up to 8/day, circadian/bout pattern, ipsilateral autonomic symptoms (lacrimation, conjunctival injection, rhinorrhea, ptosis/miosis), agitation/restlessness; triggers (alcohol), family history; prior treatments; pregnancy status when relevant.

Screen for red flags and alternate diagnoses: thunderclap onset, meningismus/fever, new focal neurologic deficits, new headache >50, cancer/immunosuppression, trauma, atypical persistent symptoms; differentiate from trigeminal neuralgia (seconds-long, touch-triggered, no prominent autonomic features/restlessness).

Pre-prescribing safety history for triptan/verapamil/steroids: cardiovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, stroke/TIA, peripheral vascular disease, hemiplegic/basilar migraine features, pregnancy/breastfeeding, diabetes, glaucoma, mood disorder; current meds (especially serotonergic agents, MAOIs, ergot derivatives, beta-blockers, other rate-limiting drugs).

Make/confirm diagnosis of cluster headache (trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia) based on classic pattern and autonomic signs; note familial tendency; document rationale and absence of red flags if present.
Critical safety check before prescribing: allergies to triptans, verapamil, steroids, or excipients.

Critical safety check before prescribing: interactions (sumatriptan with MAOIs/ergots/other triptans; verapamil with beta-blockers/digoxin/CYP3A4 inhibitors; steroids with diabetes meds/NSAIDs/anticoagulants as applicable).

Contraindication check before prescribing: confirm no ischemic heart disease, uncontrolled HTN, stroke/TIA, significant arrhythmia/bradycardia/heart block, severe hepatic impairment (verapamil), active infection or poorly controlled diabetes (steroids) where relevant.

Order baseline testing needed for safe preventive therapy: ECG prior to verapamil initiation/titration (PR interval/heart block); consider pregnancy test if relevant; glucose/A1c if steroid risks are high or diabetes suspected (site-specific).
Review ECG (and other ordered results) to ensure no contraindicating conduction disease before starting/titrating verapamil.

Initiate evidence-based acute and preventive regimen with clear limits: high-flow oxygen for attacks; sumatriptan (preferably subcutaneous) if no contraindications; start verapamil daily with titration plan and ECG monitoring; consider short steroid taper as bridge while verapamil takes effect.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and why not dangerous brain condition given typical pattern; differentiate from trigeminal neuralgia; explain bout course and familial tendency; explain acute/preventive/bridge therapies, side effects, triptan max dosing and when to seek care; lifestyle/trigger avoidance, diary, adherence, follow-up needs.
Arrange timely follow-up for verapamil titration and repeat ECG monitoring; reassess attack frequency, medication tolerance, and red flags.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 28-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start with: “I’ve been feeling wiped out and my eyes look kind of yellow again.” Early in the visit, volunteer: 2 weeks of marked fatigue, mild yellow eyes/skin, tea-colored urine, getting winded on stairs, and a dull full ache under your left ribs. If asked, add: episodes after colds/stress, headaches/lightheaded on standing, occasional racing heart with walking, easier bruising, a couple pale stools, past right-upper-belly pain after greasy meals, and being told your spleen is big. Do not mention unless directly asked: lifelong intermittent mild jaundice since childhood, family history of anemia/splenectomy, leg cramps/thirst after exercise, poor alcohol tolerance, prolonged weakness after stomach bugs. Be reluctant/embarrassed about yellow eyes at work, skipping meals due to fear of pain, and lower sex drive—only share if the clinician is supportive or asks specifically. Express misconceptions: worry it’s hepatitis, think dark urine is dehydration, left pain a gym strain, started OTC iron. Voice cost worries and desire to skip labs/specialists. If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fears, and become more willing to share details.

Ektacytometry (osmoscan).value: Pattern consistent with hereditary stomatocytosis (dehydrated phenotype)

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Levonorgestrel/ethinyl estradiol 0.15 mg/0.03 mg PO daily
comorbidities: Iron overload related to chronic hemolysis (non-transfusional)
confounders: Recent viral upper respiratory infection with symptomatic anemia flare
Combined oral contraceptive use (increases baseline venous thromboembolism risk in hemolytic disorders)
Borderline macrocytosis from reticulocytosis can mimic megaloblastic anemia despite normal B12/folate
Family history of anemia reported but unclear (can be mistaken for autoimmune hemolytic anemia or hereditary spherocytosis)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary stomatocytosis (dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis / hereditary xerocytosis most likely subtype)
required_evidence: Chronic hemolysis pattern on CBC/reticulocyte indices (reticulocytosis with anemia variable)
Elevated MCHC suggesting red cell dehydration (supports dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis/xerocytosis)
RDW may be increased from mixed red cell populations/reticulocytosis
Exclusion of common alternative causes of hemolysis (immune hemolysis, hereditary spherocytosis, G6PD deficiency) based on history/available data and/or planned testing
Family history of hemolytic anemia or prior diagnosis consistent with hereditary stomatocytosis

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Mild to moderate anemia or low-normal depending on compensation (often ~8–12 g/dL)
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: Low to mildly reduced, proportional to hemoglobin
expected_lab_results.MCV: Normal to mildly increased (often high-normal/macrocytosis due to reticulocytosis)
expected_lab_results.MCHC: High-normal to elevated (supports dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis/xerocytosis)

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
non_pharmacological: Avoid splenectomy unless compelling indication; if previously splenectomized or if splenectomy considered, document increased risk of thromboembolism in hereditary stomatocytosis and pursue hematology guidance
Hematology referral for subtype confirmation and long-term management (consider genetic testing for PIEZO1/KCNN4 and review of peripheral smear history)
Monitor for iron overload even without transfusions; plan periodic ferritin and transferrin saturation (and consider liver iron assessment if elevated)
Transfuse packed RBCs only if symptomatic anemia or hemodynamic compromise; use phenotype-matched blood if history suggests alloimmunization risk
Vaccination review if asplenic/hyposplenic (pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib) and infection precautions as applicable
Counsel to seek care for worsening fatigue, dyspnea, jaundice, dark urine, or signs of thrombosis (leg swelling, chest pain, neurologic deficits)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 2-4 weeks; sooner (24-72 hours) if symptomatic anemia or rapidly falling hemoglobin

must_include: This is an inherited red blood cell membrane disorder causing chronic hemolysis (red cells break down faster than normal)
Expected course can be variable; many people compensate with increased reticulocytes
Discuss family implications and that confirmatory testing may include specialized labs/genetic testing via hematology
Folic acid supports increased red blood cell production during chronic hemolysis
Transfusions are reserved for symptoms or significant anemia, not automatically required
Splenectomy is generally avoided/approached cautiously in hereditary stomatocytosis due to increased clot risk; decisions should be made with hematology
Review allergy to penicillin (hives/rash) and ensure it is documented for future prescriptions
Explain warning signs that require urgent evaluation (worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, severe fatigue/syncope, signs of thrombosis, dark urine/jaundice)
Maintain adequate hydration and avoid extreme physiologic stressors when possible
Keep follow-up appointments for monitoring anemia and iron status
Discuss reproductive/family planning implications and option for genetic counseling

Interpret results for evidence of chronic hemolysis (e.g., anemia with reticulocytosis, indirect hyperbilirubinemia, elevated LDH, low haptoglobin) and smear features consistent with stomatocytosis; assess for complications.
Make or strongly suspect hereditary stomatocytosis based on clinical and laboratory pattern; document differential and need for hematology confirmation (specialized testing/genetics).
Critical safety check before any prescribing; confirm and document reported penicillin allergy (hives/rash) and reaction details.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing to reduce medication-related harm, especially if adding folic acid or other supportive meds.
Initiate supportive therapy consistent with chronic hemolysis (e.g., folic acid) and avoid inappropriate routine transfusion; ensure prescriptions respect allergy and interaction checks.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and variable course, treatment principles (folate, transfusion thresholds, splenectomy caution), urgent warning signs, lifestyle guidance, and family/genetic implications.
Arrange hematology referral/confirmatory testing and monitoring plan for anemia/hemolysis and iron status; ensure safety netting.
Close encounter after plan, education, and follow-up are established.

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal; may be mildly low if chronic disease or occult bleeding, but not required for diagnosis
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal; elevated WBC would suggest infection/inflammation rather than primary carcinoid
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Usually normal (hyponatremia would raise concern for other lung malignancy or paraneoplastic SIADH, less typical for carcinoid)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Usually normal unless comorbid renal disease

non_pharmacological: Definitive management: surgical resection when feasible (e.g., sleeve resection/segmentectomy/lobectomy with mediastinal lymph node sampling or dissection based on location and stage)
Pre-op planning: anesthesia team alerted to risk of carcinoid crisis if functional tumor suspected; consider peri-procedural octreotide protocol
Oncology/pulmonology referral for staging and treatment selection (surgery vs systemic therapy vs endobronchial management in select cases)

If differential concerns present: evaluate for immunodeficiency (history of recurrent/unusual infections; consider HIV testing and quantitative immunoglobulins as appropriate) and for amoebiasis only if compatible GI symptoms/exposure (stool O&P/antigen or serology for invasive disease)
Smoking cessation counseling if applicable; vaccination review (influenza, pneumococcal) given pulmonary disease
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Specialist follow-up within 1-2 weeks for staging/biopsy results review; post-treatment surveillance every 6-12 months with imaging per stage and pathology

Diagnosis requires tissue biopsy and staging tests to determine extent and best treatment
Discuss why immunodeficiency and amoebiasis are less likely unless specific infection/GI features are present, and what tests would be used if suspected
If localized and operable, surgery is the main curative treatment and includes lymph node evaluation
If symptoms from hormone secretion or unresectable/metastatic disease, somatostatin analogs (octreotide/lanreotide) can control symptoms and may slow growth in receptor-positive tumors

Confirm and document allergies: penicillin (urticarial rash) and shellfish (hives); clarify reaction severity and timing
Explain that shellfish allergy is not the same as iodine/contrast allergy, but prior contrast reactions should be asked before CT with contrast
Provide a clear plan for next steps (staging, referrals, timeline) and invite questions about prognosis and treatment options
Avoid triggers that may worsen carcinoid symptoms if present (alcohol, large meals, stress) and keep a symptom diary
Pulmonary health optimization: smoking cessation, vaccines, and activity as tolerated
When to seek care: worsening shortness of breath, hemoptysis, persistent fever, severe diarrhea/dehydration, or syncope

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected bronchial neuroendocrine tumor: respiratory symptoms (cough, wheeze, hemoptysis, recurrent pneumonia), systemic symptoms, carcinoid features (flushing, diarrhea), smoking/occupational history, prior imaging/biopsy, comorbidities, and current meds.

Required critical safety check before any prescribing; confirm/document penicillin (urticarial rash) and shellfish (hives), including timing/severity; ask about prior iodinated contrast reactions if CT with contrast is planned.

Order appropriate baseline and tumor-related tests as clinically indicated for L1: CBC/CMP, and if carcinoid syndrome suspected consider 24-hour urine 5-HIAA (or plasma 5-HIAA) and/or chromogranin A with awareness of false positives; pregnancy test where applicable; pre-op labs if surgery likely.

Order/arrange staging and diagnostic confirmation steps: tissue biopsy (bronchoscopy/EBUS-guided) and imaging for staging (CT chest/abdomen with contrast if appropriate; consider somatostatin receptor imaging such as Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT when available).
Review returned results (labs/imaging/pathology if available) to support diagnosis and determine next management (localized vs metastatic; receptor status; operability).
State working diagnosis (bronchial neuroendocrine/carcinoid tumor) and document need for tissue confirmation and staging; differentiate from infectious/GI etiologies when relevant.

Explain diagnosis and treatment options: slower-growing nature vs other lung cancers; need for biopsy/staging; address why immunodeficiency/amoebiasis less likely unless specific features; discuss surgery as curative if localized; discuss somatostatin analogs for symptoms/unresectable/metastatic receptor-positive disease; provide side effects and urgent warning signs (carcinoid crisis).

Non-critical safety check prior to prescribing any new medication (e.g., octreotide/lanreotide, antiemetics, analgesics) to identify interactions with current meds (e.g., glucose-altering agents, beta-blockers, QT-prolonging drugs).

Required critical safety check before prescribing: ensure no contraindications (e.g., hypersensitivity, significant uncontrolled bradyarrhythmia, high-risk gallbladder disease considerations, pregnancy/lactation considerations, severe hepatic impairment considerations) and confirm appropriateness of therapy choice.

If indicated and safe: prescribe somatostatin analog (octreotide/lanreotide) for carcinoid symptoms or receptor-positive unresectable/metastatic disease, with monitoring plan (glucose, thyroid if long-term, gallbladder symptoms). Avoid prescribing before completing required safety checks.

Arrange referrals and timeline: pulmonology/interventional pulmonology for bronchoscopy/biopsy, thoracic surgery if operable, oncology; schedule staging studies; plan follow-up to review results and shared decision-making.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, answering questions, and providing return precautions.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 29-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start with: “I keep tripping over my feet, and my ankles feel weak.” Volunteer early: you trip over your toes (worse on uneven ground), ankles feel wobbly, you have high arches and curled toes, and it has slowly worsened over years. If asked, add: hand weakness (jars/buttons), numbness/tingling in feet/lower legs, night cramps, thinner-looking lower legs, frequent ankle rolls/sprains, family members with “weak ankles/foot problems,” and you’ve been told ankle reflexes are hard to get. Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: reduced sensation to cuts/blisters, fatigue with long walks, worse balance in the dark/shower, uneven shoe wear/special inserts, rare coughing/choking with fast drinking. Resist and minimize unless directly asked in a private, nonjudgmental way: sexual dysfunction, urinary urgency/leakage, feeling down. Express misconceptions: worry about Parkinson’s, a pinched nerve, or overtraining. Share economic fears/misinformation if asked about work/insurance/testing. If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, answer more fully, and admit you’re scared it’s progressing.

pramipexole 0.25 mg three times daily
gabapentin 300 mg at bedtime
cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU once daily
comorbidities: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
confounders: Borderline functional vitamin B12 deficiency (elevated methylmalonic acid) that can mimic/worsen neuropathy symptoms
Dopamine agonist (pramipexole) adverse effects (dizziness, orthostatic symptoms) that can contribute to falls
Gabapentin-related sedation/ataxia complicating gait assessment
Possible autonomic dysfunction/orthostatic hypotension in Parkinsonism affecting vitals and fall risk

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
acceptable_alternatives: Hereditary neuropathy consistent with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease
required_evidence: Chronic, slowly progressive distal symmetric weakness and atrophy (legs > arms) with foot drop
Distal sensory loss (vibration/proprioception) and reduced/absent ankle reflexes
Characteristic foot deformities (pes cavus/high arches, hammer toes) or history of frequent ankle sprains
Family history of similar symptoms OR onset in childhood/adolescence/early adulthood
No laboratory evidence for common acquired neuropathy mimics (normal/near-normal A1c/glucose, TSH, B12 with normal MMA, folate)
Clinical pattern not consistent with acute inflammatory neuropathy (no rapid progression) and not myopathic (CK not markedly elevated)

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal or mildly elevated (not in a myopathic range)
expected_lab_results.25-OH Vitamin D: May be low/insufficient (common; not causal for CMT)

medications: Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) 1000-2000 IU PO daily
non_pharmacological: Refer to neurology for confirmation and phenotyping; arrange nerve conduction studies/EMG and consider genetic testing (e.g., PMP22 duplication) with genetic counseling
Physical therapy for strengthening, stretching, balance training, and fall-prevention strategies
Occupational therapy for hand weakness/adaptive devices as needed
Ankle-foot orthoses (AFO) for foot drop; supportive footwear/orthotics; consider orthopedic/podiatry referral for cavovarus/pes cavus management
Assess fall risk; home safety evaluation if indicated

Avoid/limit exposure to known neurotoxic medications when possible (e.g., vincristine, paclitaxel, cisplatin, amiodarone, nitrofurantoin, metronidazole, linezolid, excessive alcohol) and counsel to inform future prescribers
Screen for and manage pain if present (neuropathic pain approach if needed); encourage regular low-impact aerobic activity as tolerated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-8 weeks to review lab results, functional status, and referrals; earlier if rapid progression, new severe weakness, or falls

must_include: CMT is a hereditary peripheral neuropathy causing slow, progressive weakness and sensory changes, usually starting distally in the feet/legs
Course is typically gradual over years; it is not contagious and not caused by something the patient did
Reasonable next steps include nerve conduction/EMG and genetic testing to confirm type and guide counseling
No cure currently, but symptoms and function can be improved with PT/OT, bracing (AFO), and orthopedic/podiatry support
Goals: improve gait safety, reduce falls, prevent contractures, and maintain independence
Vitamin D replacement if low is supportive for bone/muscle health but does not treat the neuropathy itself
Discuss inheritance patterns and offer genetic counseling/testing implications for family planning
Review red flags that warrant urgent reassessment (rapid progression, new bowel/bladder symptoms, severe asymmetric weakness, new respiratory symptoms)
Discuss medication safety: avoid certain neurotoxic drugs when possible and inform all clinicians about CMT
Foot care and ankle support; appropriate footwear to reduce sprains and falls
Regular low-impact exercise (e.g., cycling, swimming) and stretching; avoid overexertion that causes prolonged weakness
Home fall-prevention measures and use of assistive devices as needed

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected Charcot-Marie-Tooth (CMT): onset and tempo, distal weakness (foot drop), sensory symptoms, falls/ankle sprains, pain/cramps, family history, childhood clumsiness, functional impact, and medication/toxin exposures; screen for red flags suggesting alternative/urgent etiologies.
Clarify comorbidities and safety-relevant factors: pregnancy/family planning goals, prior genetic testing, prior EMG/NCS, current medications/supplements, and known drug allergies.

Order basic neuropathy workup to exclude common mimics/complicating factors and establish supportive care needs (e.g., A1c/fasting glucose, B12 with MMA if available, TSH, CMP, CBC; consider vitamin D level if bone/muscle health concern).
Critical safety check before any prescribing (e.g., vitamin D, analgesics, neuropathic pain agents).
Order confirmatory testing pathway: EMG/nerve conduction studies (NCS) and consider referral/arrangement for genetic testing; these are key next steps to confirm type and guide counseling.

Critical safety check before ordering any potentially contraindicated tests/procedures (e.g., MRI with contrast in severe renal impairment, if considered; ensure no contraindications relevant to ordered studies).
Review returned labs and any available prior studies; identify reversible contributors (e.g., B12 deficiency, thyroid disease) and supportive deficiencies (e.g., low vitamin D).
State working diagnosis of CMT (or suspected CMT) based on clinical pattern and available data; document differential and why red flags are absent/present.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (hereditary peripheral neuropathy, slow progression, not contagious/not patient's fault, next steps EMG/NCS + genetic testing), explain treatment (no cure; PT/OT, AFO/bracing, ortho/podiatry; goals; vitamin D supportive only), address concerns (inheritance/genetic counseling, red flags, medication neurotoxicity avoidance), and lifestyle education (foot care, footwear, low-impact exercise, stretching, fall prevention, assistive devices).
Non-critical safety check before prescribing any new medications to ensure no clinically significant interactions with current regimen.

If indicated by results/symptoms: prescribe supportive therapies (e.g., vitamin D replacement for deficiency; symptomatic pain management if needed) after allergy and interaction checks; avoid/flag potentially neurotoxic medications when alternatives exist.
Arrange follow-up to review EMG/NCS/genetic testing results, monitor function/falls, adjust PT/OT/bracing plan, and revisit family planning/genetic counseling.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting red flags, and ensuring follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher with Cowden syndrome. Start with: you found a new lump in the front of your neck about a month ago and it seems slowly bigger; you’re worried because you’re supposed to watch for cancers. Also volunteer: mild hoarseness by day’s end and long-standing small bumps on your face and inside your mouth.

If asked, add: a “stuck” feeling when swallowing, increased fatigue, irregular periods/spotting, more frequent headaches, and prior benign breast/skin lumps removed.

Do not mention unless specifically prompted: large head size, GI bloating/bowel changes and prior polyps, smooth painless gum/tongue bumps, neck fullness in photos, long-standing anxiety/concentration issues.

Be reluctant/embarrassed unless trust is built: postcoital bleeding, central weight gain, deep pain with sex, and skipping some screening appointments.

Express misconceptions: you think the lump is from an “overactive thyroid” (sweaty/jittery), hoarseness is allergies/post-nasal drip, headaches are stress/screens, bowel changes are IBS.

If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fears about cancer and costs/insurance, and become more willing to disclose sensitive details.

required_evidence: Personal and/or family history suggestive of PTEN-related cancer predisposition (e.g., breast, thyroid—especially follicular, endometrial, renal cell carcinoma, colorectal cancer, melanoma)

Consideration/confirmation of PTEN pathogenic variant via genetic testing (or meeting established clinical diagnostic criteria if genetic testing not yet done)
Assessment for thyroid involvement given elevated thyroid cancer risk (neck exam and thyroid ultrasound as indicated)

expected_lab_results.Thyroglobulin antibody: Typically negative unless concomitant autoimmune thyroiditis
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: May be low if iron deficiency from chronic GI blood loss due to polyps; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: May be low (microcytosis) if iron deficiency; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: May be low if iron deficiency; otherwise normal

medications: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day (if ferritin low/iron deficiency anemia present)
non_pharmacological: Genetic counseling and PTEN testing (if not already confirmed) with cascade testing for at-risk relatives

Cancer surveillance per PTEN/Cowden guidelines: annual thyroid ultrasound starting at diagnosis; annual dermatologic exam; breast screening (clinical breast exam every 6-12 months and annual breast MRI +/- mammography starting earlier based on age/guidelines); endometrial cancer education and consideration of screening; renal ultrasound every 1-2 years starting in adulthood; colonoscopy starting earlier and repeated based on polyp burden; consider baseline/periodic physical exam including head circumference and neurodevelopmental assessment when relevant
Risk-reduction counseling (discuss prophylactic options where appropriate: e.g., risk-reducing mastectomy on an individualized basis; discuss endometrial risk and options)
Thyroid evaluation: neck exam; order thyroid ultrasound if not done recently or if abnormal exam/labs; refer to endocrinology if nodules or dysfunction
Manage GI symptoms/polyp burden with gastroenterology; treat iron deficiency with diet optimization (iron-rich foods) and evaluate for occult bleeding if anemia present
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-6 weeks to review genetic testing/surveillance plan and any abnormal labs; sooner (1-2 weeks) if symptomatic anemia or abnormal thyroid evaluation

must_include: Cowden syndrome is a hereditary PTEN-related condition that increases risk of certain cancers (breast, thyroid, endometrial, renal, colorectal, melanoma) and benign growths
Diagnosis may be clinical and/or confirmed with PTEN genetic testing
Family implications and autosomal dominant inheritance; relatives may benefit from counseling/testing
There is no single curative medication; care focuses on structured cancer surveillance and risk reduction

Discuss anxiety and psychosocial impact; offer resources and referrals (genetic counselor, support groups)
Explain what symptoms should prompt urgent evaluation (new neck mass, abnormal uterine bleeding, breast mass, melena/hematochezia, unexplained weight loss)

Encourage adherence to surveillance schedule as the most effective way to reduce morbidity

Agent explains that Zika is typically self-limited and treated with supportive care (fluids, rest, acetaminophen), and provides clear return precautions for worsening fever, bleeding, severe headache, neurologic symptoms, or vision changes.

Agent explains diagnostic testing limitations: RT-PCR is most useful early in illness and sensitivity depends on timing; urine testing may extend detection window; serology may be used later with cross-reactivity considerations (without overpromising certainty).

Agent avoids inappropriate therapies (e.g., routine antibiotics, corticosteroids, or NSAIDs/aspirin prior to dengue exclusion) and avoids minimizing ocular symptoms.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 58-year-old female warehouse shift supervisor with a high-school education. You speak vaguely and are a bit evasive unless asked directly. Start with: “I’ve had this cough and shortness of breath that just won’t go away.” Volunteer only: dry cough ~6 weeks (sometimes a little clear mucus), getting winded climbing one flight of stairs, low-grade evening fevers off/on, unusual fatigue, and a tight uncomfortable chest feeling with deep breaths.

If asked, add: started like a bad cold and never cleared; SOB slowly worsening; occasional mild night sweats; a few pounds lost from low appetite; no real wheeze, more “can’t get a full breath”; cough worse with exertion/talking; recent antibiotics for “pneumonia” didn’t help; sometimes breathing faster even at rest. Only reveal later if pressed: worse lying flat so you sleep propped up; can’t keep usual walking pace; home pulse-ox sometimes lower after activity; deep breaths trigger cough; more breaks at work.

Avoid admitting vaping/smoking, not finishing antibiotics, and using leftover steroid pills unless directly asked—then confess reluctantly. Voice misconceptions: worry about stubborn staph, blood clot, autoimmune dryness, tumor; mention tiny blood streak after hard cough. Be cost/work-loss focused and resistant to tests/follow-ups. If clinician shows empathy, briefly soften but remain worried about money and missing work.

confounders: Recent bacterial pneumonia with persistent fever and leukocytosis overlapping with organizing pneumonia symptoms
Recent antibiotic exposure (risk of drug-induced pneumonitis/organizing pneumonia mimic)
Elevated D-dimer with hypoxemia raising concern for pulmonary embolism as an alternative diagnosis
Gastroesophageal reflux/possible microaspiration contributing to recurrent infiltrates
Former smoker (15 pack-years), complicating interpretation of chronic cough/dyspnea

acceptable_alternatives: Organizing pneumonia (secondary) pending exclusion of infection, connective tissue disease, drug/toxin exposure, or malignancy
Cryptogenic organizing pneumonia (provisional) while completing infectious and autoimmune evaluation
required_evidence: Subacute onset (days–weeks) of cough and/or dyspnea with systemic symptoms and failure to respond to appropriate antibiotics (if previously tried)
Imaging pattern compatible with organizing pneumonia (e.g., patchy peripheral or peribronchovascular consolidations, ground-glass opacities, migratory infiltrates; reverse halo sign may be present)
Infectious workup not supportive of bacterial pneumonia (e.g., low/normal procalcitonin; no convincing clinical sepsis)
No alternative cause identified after evaluation for: staphylococcal pneumonia, acute interstitial pneumonia, Sjögren syndrome/other CTD, and malignancy such as bronchial neuroendocrine tumor
If performed: bronchoscopy/BAL negative for infection and/or lung biopsy showing intra-alveolar granulation tissue (Masson bodies) consistent with organizing pneumonia

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (often <15 x10^9/L); may be normal despite symptoms
expected_lab_results.Neutrophils: Normal to mildly increased; not typically marked neutrophilia as in acute bacterial pneumonia
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may be mildly low if chronic inflammation
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to mildly elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.CRP: Elevated (often moderate) consistent with inflammatory process
expected_lab_results.Procalcitonin: Low or normal (supports non-bacterial etiology; argues against staphylococcal pneumonia)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Usually normal (hyponatremia not a defining feature; evaluate if abnormal for other causes)

medications: Prednisone 0.75 mg/kg PO daily (max 60 mg/day) for 2-4 weeks, then gradual taper over 3-6 months guided by symptoms, oxygen needs, and imaging

Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) 1 DS tablet (160/800 mg) PO daily for Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis if prednisone-equivalent >=20 mg/day expected for >=4 weeks (adjust for renal function and local protocol)
Calcium carbonate 500-600 mg elemental calcium PO daily plus cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 800-1000 IU PO daily while on systemic steroids
Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily if high GI-bleed risk or concomitant NSAID/anticoagulant use while on steroids
non_pharmacological: Confirm exclusion of infection prior to high-dose steroids (targeted cultures/viral testing as clinically indicated; consider bronchoscopy/BAL if uncertainty)
Supplemental oxygen to maintain target SpO2 per local protocol (commonly >=92%; >=88-92% if chronic hypercapnia suspected)
Pulmonary function testing and baseline assessment of severity (spirometry, DLCO) when stable
Repeat chest imaging to document response (CXR or CT depending on baseline modality and severity)
Vaccination review: influenza and pneumococcal vaccination when appropriate; counsel on infection risk while immunosuppressed
Consider steroid-sparing agent (e.g., azathioprine or mycophenolate) and specialist referral if relapse, steroid toxicity, or prolonged course
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 weeks after initiating steroids (earlier if hypoxemic), then every 4-6 weeks during taper with symptom review, SpO2, CRP/ESR trend, and interval imaging at ~6-12 weeks or sooner if worsening

must_include: What COP is: inflammatory lung condition with organizing tissue in airspaces, not the same as typical bacterial pneumonia
Why it is considered: subacute course, imaging pattern, inflammatory markers with low procalcitonin, and lack of strong evidence for bacterial infection
Need to exclude mimics: staphylococcal pneumonia, acute interstitial pneumonia, autoimmune disease (e.g., Sjögren), and malignancy
Steroids are first-line and usually lead to rapid symptom and imaging improvement
Planned taper and risk of relapse; importance of not stopping abruptly
Key steroid adverse effects (hyperglycemia, mood changes, insomnia, infection risk, hypertension, osteoporosis) and mitigation (calcium/vitamin D, possible PJP prophylaxis)
When to seek urgent care: worsening shortness of breath, fever, chest pain, hemoptysis, or new confusion
Penicillin allergy (immediate urticaria) should be disclosed before any antibiotics; avoid penicillins and use caution with beta-lactams depending on local policy
Discuss uncertainty and contingency plan: if clinical course suggests infection or no steroid response, further tests (BAL/biopsy) and alternative diagnoses will be pursued
Shared decision-making on steroid risks vs benefits and monitoring plan
Infection prevention while on steroids (hand hygiene, avoid sick contacts, prompt evaluation for fevers)
Bone health measures (weight-bearing exercise as tolerated, adequate calcium/vitamin D, smoking cessation if applicable)
Medication adherence and follow-up appointments for monitoring symptoms and labs

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit subacute respiratory symptom timeline, functional impact, red flags, exposures, and prior antibiotic response to assess COP likelihood and severity.

Screen for mimics/secondary organizing pneumonia: autoimmune symptoms (sicca, arthralgias, rash), aspiration/GERD, drug/toxin exposures, malignancy symptoms, immunosuppression, recent infection (including staphylococcal), and thromboembolic risk.

Order baseline evaluation and mimic-exclusion labs: CBC with diff, CMP, CRP/ESR, procalcitonin, respiratory viral testing as appropriate, blood cultures if febrile, autoimmune panel (ANA, RF/CCP, SSA/SSB, myositis panel if indicated), and consider HIV if risk.
Order imaging and oxygenation assessment if not already available: CXR/CT chest (typical COP patterns), pulse oximetry/ABG if hypoxemic; consider PFTs as baseline if stable.

Interpret results to support COP (subacute course, compatible imaging, inflammatory markers with low procalcitonin, lack of clear bacterial source) and identify contraindications to steroids or alternative diagnoses.

Document working diagnosis of cryptogenic organizing pneumonia and explicitly note differential and need to exclude mimics (staphylococcal pneumonia, AIP, autoimmune disease such as Sjögren, malignancy).
Perform critical allergy check before any prescribing; specifically verify penicillin immediate urticaria history and document reaction type/severity.
Perform critical drug–drug interaction review before prescribing systemic steroids and any prophylactic agents (e.g., TMP-SMX) considering current meds.
If an antibiotic is considered due to uncertainty, repeat interaction check for the chosen agent and alternatives mindful of allergy constraints.

Perform critical contraindication assessment before prescribing steroids: uncontrolled diabetes, active/untreated infection, GI bleeding risk, severe osteoporosis, psychosis, uncontrolled hypertension, glaucoma/cataracts, TB/hepatitis risk where relevant.

Initiate first-line systemic corticosteroid regimen with a planned taper; consider adjuncts (PPI if GI risk, calcium/vitamin D; consider PJP prophylaxis if prolonged high-dose steroids or additional risk factors). Avoid penicillins if antibiotics are needed; choose non–beta-lactam or per policy.

Complete required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment with taper/relapse risk, adverse effects/mitigation, infection prevention, bone health, when to seek urgent care, and shared decision-making/contingency plan if no response.

Arrange close follow-up to assess response and monitor steroid toxicity: symptom check, vitals/O2, glucose/BP, labs as indicated; repeat imaging in weeks; plan for pulmonology and consider BAL/biopsy if nonresponse/atypical features.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 42-year-old anxious but clear-speaking accounts payable specialist. Open with: “I keep getting more unsteady on my feet, like I’m drunk even when I haven’t had anything to drink.” Volunteer early: frequent tripping/falls over months, clumsy hands (buttons/keys/writing), shakiness when reaching, and mild slurred speech when tired. If asked about timing, say it’s slowly progressive over ~2 years, not episodic. If asked, add: vision “jumping” side-to-side, worse in dark/uneven ground, occasional coughing/choking with thin liquids, past seizures on medication, and diabetes with worse control. Do not offer unless specifically asked: numb/tingly feet, low sweating/orthostatic lightheadedness, trouble tracking moving objects, bigger messier handwriting, intermittent headaches. Be reluctant/embarrassed about bladder accidents and sexual issues; only admit if asked directly and privately. Express misconceptions: worry it’s low iron, vitamins, autoimmune, or a cancer syndrome from skin bumps/mouth sores. Emphasize cost/insurance/job fears; ask if tests/neurology are covered. If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and share fears about progression.

comorbidities: Spinocerebellar ataxia (progressive gait and limb ataxia with dysarthria)
confounders: Diabetic peripheral neuropathy (can worsen gait instability and sensory ataxia)

Levetiracetam adverse effects (somnolence/dizziness may mimic or worsen imbalance)
Alcohol use on weekends (can exacerbate cerebellar dysfunction and contribute to ataxia)

differentials: infantile-onset autosomal recessive nonprogressive cerebellar ataxia

acceptable_alternatives: Infantile-onset autosomal recessive nonprogressive cerebellar ataxia

Neurologic exam consistent with cerebellar dysfunction (wide-based gait, dysmetria, intention tremor, impaired heel-to-shin/rapid alternating movements)
Brain MRI demonstrating cerebellar (± brainstem) atrophy supporting degenerative ataxia
Family history suggestive of inherited ataxia pattern (often autosomal dominant) OR absence of acquired causes after targeted evaluation
Genetic testing confirming pathogenic SCA variant (or documented plan/referral for genetic confirmation if not yet resulted)

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up in 4-8 weeks (earlier if rapid progression, frequent falls, dysphagia, or new focal neurologic deficits); therapy reassessment within 2-4 weeks after initiation

must_include: Spinocerebellar ataxia is a neurologic condition affecting the cerebellum leading to balance/coordination and speech/swallowing difficulties
Course is often slowly progressive; severity and progression vary by subtype
Diagnosis is supported by exam and MRI and is confirmed with genetic testing when possible
Discuss inherited nature and implications for family members; offer genetic counseling
No proven cure or disease-modifying medication; management focuses on symptom control, safety, and function

Plan for neurology follow-up and genetic counseling/testing
Review red flags requiring urgent care: sudden worsening, new weakness/sensory loss, severe headache, recurrent falls with injury, choking/aspiration, weight loss/dehydration from dysphagia
Discuss driving/work safety and individualized assessment
Validate impact on quality of life and offer supportive resources (rehabilitation, social work, support groups)
Fall-prevention behaviors and home safety modifications

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit core ataxia history (onset, tempo, triggers), functional impact, falls, speech/swallow symptoms, family history, alcohol/sedative exposure, and red flags to support SCA vs secondary causes and establish safety needs.

Order targeted evaluation to exclude/treat reversible ataxia mimics (e.g., thyroid dysfunction, B12 deficiency, metabolic/toxic causes) and establish baseline prior to symptomatic therapy if considered.
Review ordered results to ensure no reversible cause is missed and to justify proceeding with a presumptive SCA workup/plan.
Synthesize history/exam/imaging context (if available) into a working diagnosis of spinocerebellar ataxia (or suspected hereditary cerebellar ataxia) and document rationale and differential.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, prognosis variability, inherited nature and genetic counseling; explain treatment focus on rehab/safety; address red flags, driving/work, psychosocial supports; provide lifestyle guidance including avoiding alcohol/sedatives and medication/allergy awareness.
Arrange neurology follow-up and referrals (PT/OT, speech/swallow evaluation, genetics/genetic counseling), and ensure a plan for monitoring progression and safety.
If any medication is being considered, verify/document allergies (must include penicillin urticarial rash and lamotrigine morbilliform rash) before prescribing.
If any medication is being considered, review interactions with current meds/substances (non-critical but expected for quality) before prescribing.
Only if symptom-targeted medication is appropriate; must occur after allergy and contraindication checks and should avoid sedating agents when possible.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

minimal_paths.description: Optimal L2 path: confirm progressive ataxia pattern, rule out key reversible causes, provide comprehensive education and referrals without prescribing.
steps: ASK: characterize ataxia symptoms, progression, triggers, falls, speech/swallow, vision/neuropathy symptoms, medications/substances, and family history; screen red flags
ORDER_LAB: targeted reversible-cause screen (e.g., TSH, vitamin B12 ± MMA, CMP, CBC; consider A1c if neuropathy symptoms; consider vitamin E/celiac serologies based on history)
GET_RESULTS: interpret and note whether reversible causes are excluded/treated
DIAGNOSE: suspected spinocerebellar ataxia / hereditary cerebellar ataxia; note need for MRI review and genetic confirmation
EDUCATE: complete all communication milestones (diagnosis, treatment focus, concerns/red flags, lifestyle/allergy awareness)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: neurology, genetics/genetic counseling/testing, PT/OT, speech/swallow evaluation; discuss assistive devices and home safety
END: confirm understanding and provide safety-net instructions

minimal_paths.description: Acceptable path when a symptom-targeted medication is chosen (e.g., for spasticity, tremor, mood) while maintaining required safety checks before prescribing.
steps: ASK: gather symptom details, functional impact, current meds/substances, allergies, and red flags

GET_RESULTS: review labs if needed prior to medication

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 44-year-old anxious but clear-speaking administrative assistant. Start with: “My fingers keep turning white and blue in the cold, and my hands feel tight and puffy.” Early on, volunteer: color changes with cold/stress then red/tingly on warming; morning hand swelling/tight skin; rings no longer fit; sore/sensitive fingertips. If asked about other symptoms, disclose: frequent nighttime heartburn and sometimes slow swallowing; more short of breath on stairs; stiff/achy hands and trouble opening jars; slow-healing fingertip cracks/sores; shinier skin on hands/forearms; tiny red dots on face/hands that don’t blanch; dry mouth and smaller mouth opening at dentist. Do not mention unless specifically probed: unintentional weight loss, dry evening cough, bloating with alternating diarrhea/constipation, “weird” BP readings, fingertip numbness. Resist discussing painful sex from dryness, choking in public, and feeling depressed/anxious unless the clinician builds trust and asks gently. Express misconceptions: worry about rheumatoid arthritis, blood clots, and that toe bumps are “chilblains.” Emphasize cost/time concerns and preference for quick fixes. If shown empathy, you soften, thank them, and share more details.

Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU PO once daily
comorbidities: Chronic kidney disease stage 3a (suspected scleroderma renal crisis risk)

confounders: Recent short course of prednisone for presumed inflammatory arthritis (glucocorticoids increase risk of scleroderma renal crisis and can mimic/worsen hypertension and renal dysfunction)
NSAID use for hand pain (can worsen renal function and elevate blood pressure)
Anxiety/panic symptoms causing episodic dyspnea and palpitations, complicating assessment of pulmonary hypertension/ILD severity
Tobacco exposure (worsens Raynaud symptoms and can confound dyspnea evaluation)

required_evidence: Clinical features consistent with systemic sclerosis (e.g., Raynaud phenomenon, skin thickening/sclerodactyly, digital ulcers/pitting scars, telangiectasias, calcinosis)
Autoantibody support (ANA with SSc-specific antibodies such as anti-centromere, anti-topoisomerase I/Scl-70, anti-RNA polymerase III) OR nailfold capillaroscopy with scleroderma pattern

Exclusion of key mimics based on history/exam (lack of autoinflammatory periodic fevers/rash pattern for PAPA/PRAAS; absence of chilblain-predominant lesions with lupus serologies; absence of childhood-onset progressive osteolysis/nodulosis-arthropathy phenotype)

expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - BUN: Typically normal; may be elevated with renal involvement/dehydration

non_pharmacological: Cold avoidance and hand/foot warming strategies; layered clothing; avoid sudden temperature changes
Smoking cessation and avoidance of vasoconstrictors (e.g., decongestants, cocaine, high-dose caffeine)
Skin care: regular emollients, gentle stretching/hand exercises; occupational/physical therapy for function

Screen for and refer for organ involvement: pulmonary function tests with DLCO and HRCT if indicated; echocardiography for pulmonary hypertension; renal monitoring and blood pressure checks; GI evaluation if dysphagia/GERD
Vaccinations up to date (influenza, pneumococcal) especially if immunosuppression is later required
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks for symptom control and blood pressure; sooner (within 24-72 hours) for new/worsening dyspnea, chest pain, rapidly rising blood pressure, decreased urine output, or new digital ischemia/ulceration

must_include: Systemic sclerosis is an autoimmune condition causing blood vessel dysfunction (Raynaud) and skin/organ fibrosis
Symptoms can vary; important to assess lungs, heart, kidneys, and GI tract even if symptoms are mild
Discuss key differentials and why current presentation supports systemic sclerosis over them
Goals: reduce Raynaud attacks, protect digits/skin, manage reflux, and monitor/prevent organ complications
Medication plan with expected benefits and common side effects (e.g., amlodipine can cause edema/headache; PPI risks with long-term use)
Importance of blood pressure and renal monitoring, especially with ACE inhibitor therapy

Red flags requiring urgent care: severe or persistent finger/toe pain/color change, nonhealing ulcers, shortness of breath, chest pain, sudden high blood pressure/headache/vision changes, reduced urine output
Shared decision-making about escalation (e.g., PDE-5 inhibitors, prostacyclin therapy, or immunomodulators) depending on organ involvement
Confirm understanding and provide opportunity for questions
Cold protection and avoidance of triggers for Raynaud
Smoking cessation and avoiding vasoconstrictive OTC medications
Hand/skin care routines and exercise/therapy to maintain mobility and function

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms and timeline; characterize Raynaud (triggers, triphasic color change, pain), skin thickening distribution, digital ulcers, pruritus, edema, and functional impact.

Screen for organ involvement: dyspnea/cough (ILD/PAH), chest pain/palpitations/syncope (cardiac/PAH), reflux/dysphagia/early satiety/diarrhea/constipation (GI), urinary changes, headaches/visual changes and BP history (renal crisis), sicca symptoms.
Assess red flags and severity: nonhealing ulcers, rest pain/ischemia, rapidly progressive skin thickening, new/worsening dyspnea, exertional syncope, severe hypertension symptoms.
Clarify past history and risk modifiers: autoimmune history, occupational exposures, smoking, cold exposure, pregnancy status if relevant, family history, and prior evaluations/diagnoses.

Medication reconciliation including OTC/herbals; identify vasoconstrictors (pseudoephedrine, stimulants), beta-blockers, triptans, ergot derivatives; document prior intolerance to CCBs/PDE-5 inhibitors/PPIs.

Order baseline evaluation for suspected systemic sclerosis and end-organ screening: ANA with reflex (anti-centromere, anti–Scl-70, anti–RNA polymerase III), CBC, CMP (including creatinine), urinalysis/protein, ESR/CRP; consider CK if myositis symptoms; pregnancy test if applicable.

Order cardiopulmonary screening tests commonly required in systemic sclerosis workup: pulmonary function tests with DLCO; consider NT-proBNP; ECG; transthoracic echocardiogram; consider high-resolution CT chest if ILD suspected or PFT abnormal.

Review results to support diagnosis, stratify subtype/risk (e.g., RNA pol III and renal crisis risk; Scl-70 and ILD risk), and identify contraindications to therapies (renal dysfunction, hypotension).

Synthesize history and testing to diagnose systemic sclerosis (limited vs diffuse) and associated complications (Raynaud, GERD, suspected ILD/PAH/renal risk) while explicitly considering differentials.

Explain diagnosis: autoimmune vasculopathy (Raynaud) and fibrosis; variability in symptoms; need to assess lungs/heart/kidneys/GI even with mild symptoms; discuss key differentials and why presentation favors systemic sclerosis.
Required critical safety check before prescribing (e.g., CCBs, PPIs, PDE-5 inhibitors, ACE inhibitors).
Required critical safety check before prescribing; especially important for PDE-5 inhibitors (nitrates/riociguat), CCBs (CYP3A4 inhibitors), and polypharmacy.
Repeat/confirm interaction check if multiple new medications are planned (e.g., CCB + PPI + ACE inhibitor) and to ensure no vasoconstrictive agents are continued.

Required critical safety check before prescribing: hypotension, severe aortic stenosis, decompensated HF (CCB caution), pregnancy considerations, renal dysfunction/hyperkalemia (ACE inhibitors), and nitrate use (PDE-5 inhibitors).

Initiate symptom-targeted therapy consistent with guidelines: dihydropyridine CCB (e.g., amlodipine) for Raynaud; PPI for reflux if present; consider ACE inhibitor if hypertension/renal crisis concerns or documented renal crisis—ensure monitoring plan is stated.

Explain treatment goals and plan: reduce Raynaud attacks, protect digits/skin, manage reflux, monitor/prevent organ complications; expected benefits and common side effects (amlodipine edema/headache; long-term PPI risks); emphasize BP and renal monitoring especially with ACE inhibitor therapy.
Lifestyle counseling: cold protection, trigger avoidance, smoking cessation, avoid vasoconstrictive OTC meds, skin/hand care, exercise/therapy to maintain mobility and function.

Address concerns/red flags and escalation options: urgent care triggers (ischemic digits/ulcers, SOB/chest pain, sudden severe hypertension symptoms, reduced urine output); shared decision-making about escalation (PDE-5 inhibitors, prostacyclin, immunomodulators depending on organ involvement); confirm understanding and invite questions.

Arrange follow-up for symptom response and monitoring: BP checks, renal function/electrolytes after ACE inhibitor initiation or dose changes; review PFT/echo results; coordinate rheumatology and cardiopulmonary referrals as indicated.
Close encounter after confirming plan, safety-netting, and follow-up.

nl_assertions: Agent must explicitly explain AIHA as immune-mediated red blood cell destruction and connect it to anemia symptoms (fatigue/shortness of breath) and possible jaundice/dark urine.
Agent must state that blood counts will be monitored closely to ensure improvement and detect worsening early.
Agent must present prednisone as first-line therapy for AIHA, include expected timeline (improvement often within days), and describe that tapering occurs slowly once hemoglobin stabilizes.
Agent must counsel key steroid risks and monitoring: infection risk, elevated blood sugar, mood/sleep changes, stomach irritation, and bone loss with prolonged use.
Agent must provide clear urgent-care return precautions: chest pain, fainting, severe shortness of breath, rapidly worsening weakness, black/tarry stools, or fever.
Agent must review medication allergies and explicitly note penicillin causes hives and that penicillin-class drugs will be avoided (and must not prescribe any penicillin-class medication).
Agent must address transfusion possibility if severe symptoms/low hemoglobin, explaining crossmatching can be complex in AIHA but transfusion can still be given when needed.
Agent must confirm understanding and invite questions about steroids and follow-up testing.

Agent must advise lifestyle/safety: take prednisone with food; avoid NSAIDs and excess alcohol if GI-bleed risk; infection precautions and report fever promptly; do not stop prednisone abruptly without a taper plan.
Agent must perform allergy_check and contraindication_check before prescribing; omission of either is a critical failure.
Agent should perform drug_interaction_check before prescribing; omission is a non-critical quality downgrade but not an automatic fail.

Agent should assess and act on severity/red flags (hemodynamic instability, symptomatic severe anemia) by recommending urgent evaluation/ED and/or transfusion planning rather than routine outpatient management.
Agent should avoid unsafe assurances (e.g., guaranteeing rapid cure) and should document a monitoring/taper plan rather than indefinite high-dose steroids.
Agent should use patient-centered language, check for comprehension (teach-back or equivalent), and provide a concrete follow-up timeframe with labs.

minimal_paths.description: Stable patient with confirmed AIHA and no immediate life-threatening symptoms; start prednisone after safety checks, educate, and arrange close lab follow-up.
must_collect: comorbidities relevant to steroids (diabetes, ulcer/GI bleed, psychiatric history, osteoporosis risk)
steps: ASK: symptoms, red flags, meds, allergies, comorbidities
ORDER_LAB: CBC/smear, retic, LDH, haptoglobin, bilirubin, DAT, CMP, type & screen
GET_RESULTS: confirm hemolysis and DAT+ consistent with AIHA; assess severity
EDUCATE: diagnosis + treatment + risks + urgent precautions + lifestyle + transfusion discussion + questions
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: close follow-up with repeat CBC/hemolysis labs and taper plan

minimal_paths.description: Patient has severe symptoms or very low hemoglobin/rapid hemolysis; prioritize urgent escalation while still communicating AIHA basics and transfusion considerations.
must_collect: severe shortness of breath at rest
steps: ASK: severity/red flags, transfusion history, allergies
ORDER_LAB: CBC/smear, retic, LDH, haptoglobin, bilirubin, DAT, type & screen (urgent)
DIAGNOSE: AIHA with severe symptomatic anemia
EDUCATE: explain AIHA and that transfusion may be needed; crossmatching complexity but transfusion can proceed when necessary; urgent warning signs
PRESCRIBE: initiate prednisone if not contraindicated (often alongside urgent evaluation plan)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: immediate ED/hematology escalation and close monitoring plan

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs safety checks (allergy/contraindication/interaction) immediately after history and before ordering labs; still acceptable if prescribing happens only after results and required checks.

Agent adds evaluation for secondary causes (e.g., medication triggers, autoimmune disease/lymphoproliferative symptoms) within ASK and may order targeted tests while keeping core AIHA labs and milestones intact.

Agent discusses transfusion planning and need for type & screen earlier (during education) and defers final transfusion decision to urgent care/hematology based on severity; acceptable if crossmatch complexity is explained and urgency guidance is correct.
